title: Kate Shemirani, nurse turned conspiracy influencer
gist: Prospect profiles Kate Shemirani, an anti-vaccine influencer with nearly 200,000 social-media followers who rejected chemotherapy for her own 2012 breast cancer in favor of Gerson therapy and coffee enemas; her daughter Paloma later refused chemotherapy for lymphoma and died in 2024, with a 2025 inquest finding Shemirani 'more than minimally' contributed to that death, yet she remains unrepentant.

insights.claim: Kate Shemirani built a social-media following of nearly 200,000 across platforms after the Nursing and Midwifery Council revoked her nursing licence in 2021 for actively discouraging mask-wearing, social distancing, and vaccination during the Covid pandemic.
insights.speaker: Kathryn Schoon
insights.so_what: A case study in how professional deplatforming/regulatory sanction fails to shrink health-misinformation reach — relevant to platform-moderation and public-health-messaging risk assessments.
insights.quote: actively discouraged people from wearing masks, adhering to social distancing, and taking vaccinations
insights.locator: Shemirani had her nursing licence revoked

insights.claim: After being diagnosed with aggressive breast cancer in 2012 and undergoing a double mastectomy, Shemirani refused chemotherapy and had her breast implants removed, instead using mistletoe injections, Gerson therapy, 13 homemade juices a day, coffee enemas, a vegan diet, and more than 100 supplements, which she credits with detoxifying her body of cancer.
insights.speaker: Kathryn Schoon
insights.so_what: Illustrates the alternative-medicine product ecosystem (juicing, supplements, detox protocols) that anti-establishment health influencers commercialize and promote to followers.
insights.locator: Instead, she turned to holistic methods

insights.claim: Shemirani's daughter Paloma, a Cambridge graduate diagnosed with Non-Hodgkin lymphoma in 2023, refused chemotherapy that doctors said gave her a high chance of survival and died seven months later in July 2024; a 2025 inquest concluded Paloma was 'adversely influenced' by her mother, whose care was ruled 'incomprehensible but not unlawful killing.'
insights.speaker: Kathryn Schoon
insights.so_what: A coroner-documented instance of parental health misinformation contributing to a young adult's preventable death — evidentiary material for debates on regulating health misinformation and platform/parental liability.

insights.claim: In April 2024, Paloma's twin brother Gabriel, older brother Sebastian, and her former boyfriend launched High Court proceedings to assess whether Paloma could make treatment decisions independent of her mother's influence, but the case lapsed unresolved when Paloma died before it concluded.
insights.speaker: Kathryn Schoon
insights.so_what: Highlights a gap in mental-capacity safeguarding mechanisms that could not intervene fast enough to protect an at-risk adult — relevant to capacity-law and safeguarding-reform policy tracking.

insights.claim: Despite the inquest's findings and her estrangement from her son Gabriel, Shemirani remains unrepentant, alleging without evidence that Paloma was 'non-consensually given chemotherapy' and responding evasively when asked if she still has cancer, saying 'Everybody has cancer.'
insights.speaker: Kathryn Schoon
insights.so_what: Signals that legal and reputational consequences are unlikely to reduce this influencer's continued public activity or audience engagement, keeping the misinformation pipeline active.
insights.locator: I ask whether she still has cancer
